title: Ransomware negotiator hired to represent victims was working for the attackers
gist: A DigitalMint ransomware negotiator, Angelo Martino, was sentenced to 70 months for secretly feeding confidential client negotiation data to BlackCat/ALPHV attackers to inflate ransoms -- and for going further, obtaining his own affiliate account to directly extort additional victims -- exposing a structural conflict-of-interest risk in the incident-response industry.

insights.claim: Angelo Martino, a ransomware negotiator for DigitalMint, was sentenced to 70 months in prison for colluding with BlackCat (ALPHV) ransomware attackers to maximize ransoms paid by the very clients who hired him to reduce them.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Establishes a concrete legal precedent and sentencing benchmark (70-87 month guideline range) for insider collusion in the ransomware-negotiation industry.

insights.claim: Five victims Martino was hired to help paid over $75 million to ransomware affiliates, with ransom demands inflated in part by confidential negotiation information -- including insurance policy limits and internal negotiating positions -- that Martino leaked without the victims' knowledge.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Quantifies the direct financial harm attributable to the scheme and shows how privileged insurance/negotiation data becomes a direct extortion multiplier when leaked.
insights.quote: Five of the victims whom Martino was supposed to help paid over $75 million to ransomware affiliates, including likely millions of dollars in ransom demands inflated as a result of the confidential information provided by Martino.
insights.locator: opening paragraph, quoting US sentencing memorandum

insights.claim: Martino used a hidden 'intermediary chat' channel within the BlackCat negotiation panel -- accessible only to himself and BlackCat negotiators/affiliates -- to pass along victims' confidential insurance limits and negotiating positions, specifically to maximize the ransom BlackCat could extract.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Shows the precise mechanism of the fraud: a covert communication channel invisible to the employer, which is a detectable control gap for incident-response firms to audit for going forward.
insights.quote: The purpose of these intermediary chat communications was to maximize the ransom payments paid by those victims to the BlackCat actors.

insights.claim: Individual ransom payments in the scheme ranged from $213,000 to $26.8 million between April and September 2023, hitting victims in hospitality, nonprofit, financial services, retail, and medical sectors.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Illustrates the wide range of ransom sizes and victim sectors exposed to negotiator-level insider risk.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Victims of the scheme paid ransoms'

insights.claim: Beyond leaking information, Martino obtained his own BlackCat affiliate account in May 2023, shared it with two co-conspirators (a fellow DigitalMint negotiator and a Sygnia incident manager), and the three directly deployed BlackCat ransomware against five additional victims, successfully extorting $1.2 million from a medical device company.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Shows the negotiator crossed from passive collusion into active criminal operation of the ransomware itself, escalating both the harm and the sentencing exposure.
insights.quote: Martino further used his knowledge of ransomware and connections with ransomware actors to secure an affiliate account for himself, Martin, and Goldberg with ALPHV BlackCat.

insights.claim: Martino used cryptocurrency proceeds from the scheme to buy two houses in Florida, a boat, and several vehicles before the FBI seized his remaining crypto holdings.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Illustrates the personal financial scale of the scheme and the asset-forfeiture consequences now attached to it.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Martino received millions of dollars in cryptocurrency'

insights.claim: BlackCat/ALPHV, the ransomware group at the center of the scheme, previously caused the February 2024 Change Healthcare outage and hit hundreds of other victims; despite an FBI decryption tool and website seizures in December 2023, its affiliate structure remained active enough for this collusion scheme to run through 2023.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Shows law-enforcement disruption actions did not fully dismantle BlackCat's operational capability, underscoring persistent ransomware risk for the healthcare sector.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Ransomware encrypts and steals data'

insights.claim: The US government still offers rewards of up to $10 million for information on BlackCat administrators and affiliates, indicating the group's leadership remains largely unresolved even after this prosecution.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Signals ongoing enforcement risk and bounty-driven intelligence gathering that could surface further prosecutions tied to this ransomware family.

insights.claim: DigitalMint says it maintained industry-standard controls including background checks and compliance procedures, but Martino evaded them by using unauthorized, separate communication channels accessible only to him and the attackers -- illustrating that standard vendor due diligence can be insufficient against a rogue insider with direct access to attacker infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: A cautionary data point for enterprises vetting incident-response and ransomware-negotiation vendors: contractual controls may not catch insiders using out-of-band channels with attackers.
insights.quote: DigitalMint maintained controls consistent with industry standards, including background checks and compliance procedures, but Martino intentionally hid his conduct from the company, including through separate, unauthorized communication channels that the government’s filings describe as accessible only to Martino and the BlackCat negotiators and affiliates,
insights.locator: section '"also an unknowing victim"', DigitalMint statement
